You may choose from:
- **Normal** (Gaussian): For variables that can be positive or negative, symmetric around the mean

3. Estimate the following quantities:

Type of parameter being estimated: population‐level mean of the midpoint of posted salary ranges for data science and adjacent jobs in the US.

Choice of distribution: A Normal distribution is appropriate for expressing uncertainty about a continuous, unbounded (above zero) population mean. Although the true value must be positive, the mean is far from zero, and a normal prior with negligible probability below zero is acceptable. Symmetric uncertainty around the best guess is natural when modeling parameter uncertainty.

Known facts and data sources:
• Glassdoor and Payscale report median base salaries for data scientists around \$110k–\$120k.  
• Typical posted ranges: data analyst (\$50k–\$80k), data scientist (\$100k–\$140k), senior (\$120k–\$180k), ML engineer (\$110k–\$160k).  
• Weighted midpoint calculation across role mix yields ≈\$116k.  

Key influencing factors:
• Role mix (analyst vs. scientist vs. engineer vs. senior)  
• Geographic weighting (tech hubs vs. smaller markets)  
• Company size/sector  
• Market trends and inflation  

Limitations and uncertainties:
• Publication bias in online postings (higher-paying firms may be overrepresented)  
• Time‐trends (salaries rising over recent years)  
• Variation in role definitions and benefits  
• We’re uncertain about the true population mean but expect it lies in a relatively narrow band around \$115k.

Subgroup/time variation:
• Entry‐level roles drag the mean down, senior/manager roles push it up.  
• Salaries have trended upward ~3–5% annually; our belief is for “current” (2025) levels.

Best guess (mean): \$115,000  
Standard deviation: \$10,000 (implying 95% belief interval roughly \$95k–\$135k)

Justification of stdev magnitude:
• A \$10k stdev reflects moderate confidence: we allow room for the mean to be as low as ≈\$95k or as high as ≈\$135k but consider values outside that range unlikely.  
• This stdev captures uncertainty about the population mean, not the dispersion of individual job salaries (which is much larger).

Edge cases considered:
• If entry‐level roles dominated postings, the mean midpoint could dip toward \$90k–\$100k.  
• If senior roles were overweighted, it could rise toward \$140k–\$150k.  
• Our chosen uncertainty range accommodates these possibilities with low but non‐zero probability.
</parameter_estimation_process>

<std_dev>10000</std_dev>

A standard deviation of \$10k yields a 95% plausible range of \$95k to \$135k for the true mean, reflecting moderate uncertainty about role mix, geographic weighting, and market trends.
</justification>
